bodies.name: Mimas
bodies.description: Mimas is a small inner moon, about 396 km in diameter, famously resembling the Death Star due to its massive crater Herschel. Its surface is heavily cratered, icy, and geologically inactive. Mimas’s gravitational interactions influence nearby ring structures and other moons.
bodies.discoveredBy: William Herschel

bodies.name: Encelade
bodies.description: Enceladus is one of Saturn’s most famous moons, roughly 504 km across, known for its icy surface and active geysers. The plumes of water vapor and ice hint at a subsurface ocean, making it a prime candidate for extraterrestrial life. Its orbit and internal heating are influenced by tidal interactions with Saturn and nearby moons.
bodies.discoveredBy: William Herschel

bodies.name: Téthys
bodies.description: Tethys is a large inner moon, roughly 1,062 km in diameter, with a heavily cratered icy surface. It features a prominent canyon called Ithaca Chasma. Its orbit is influenced by gravitational interactions with nearby moons.
bodies.discoveredBy: Jean-Dominique Cassini

bodies.name: Dioné
bodies.description: Dione is a small inner moon of Saturn, about 112 km in diameter, orbiting just outside the dense F-ring region. Its surface is heavily cratered and icy, indicating little geological activity. Dione’s orbit is slightly perturbed by nearby moons like Enceladus and Tethys.
bodies.discoveredBy: Jean-Dominique Cassini

bodies.name: Rhéa
bodies.description: Rhea is Saturn’s second-largest moon, about 1,527 km in diameter, with a heavily cratered icy surface. It may have a tenuous atmosphere composed of oxygen and carbon dioxide. Rhea orbits Saturn at a relatively large distance and influences nearby ring particles.
bodies.discoveredBy: Jean-Dominique Cassini

bodies.name: Titan
bodies.description: Titan is Saturn’s largest moon, about 5,150 km in diameter, with a thick nitrogen-rich atmosphere. Its surface has lakes of liquid methane and ethane, and evidence of weather and surface processes. Titan orbits Saturn in a nearly circular path influenced by the planet’s gravity.
bodies.discoveredBy: Christian Huygens

bodies.name: Hypérion
bodies.description: Has an irregular, potato-like shape and a porous, sponge-like surface.
bodies.discoveredBy: William Cranch Bond, George Phillips Bond

bodies.name: Japet
bodies.description: Iapetus is a large, distinctive moon of Saturn, about 1,469 km in diameter, known for its striking two-tone coloration with one dark hemisphere and one bright hemisphere. Its surface is heavily cratered, with prominent equatorial ridges and ancient terrain. Iapetus orbits Saturn at a relatively large distance (~3.56 million km) in a slightly inclined, synchronous orbit.
bodies.discoveredBy: Jean-Dominique Cassini

bodies.name: Phœbé
bodies.description: Phoebe is a large, irregular outer moon of Saturn, about 213 km in diameter, with a dark, heavily cratered surface. It orbits Saturn in a retrograde, distant, and inclined path, suggesting it was captured from the Kuiper Belt rather than forming with the planet. Phoebe is considered the largest and most massive of Saturn’s irregular moons.
bodies.discoveredBy: William Henry Pickering

bodies.name: Janus
bodies.description: Janus is a medium-sized moon, roughly 179 km across, that shares a co-orbital relationship with Epimetheus. The two moons swap orbits approximately every four years in a rare orbital dance. Janus’s surface is heavily cratered and icy.
bodies.discoveredBy: Audouin Dollfus

bodies.name: Epiméthée
bodies.description: Epimetheus is a small, irregular moon about 116 km in diameter that shares a co-orbital relationship with Janus. It slowly swaps orbits with Janus every few years, a rare phenomenon in the Solar System. Its surface is heavily cratered and covered in water ice.
bodies.discoveredBy: Richard L. Walker

bodies.name: Hélène
bodies.description: Helene is a small Trojan moon of Saturn, sharing its orbit with the larger moon Dione. Measuring about 36 km across, it resides at Dione’s leading Lagrange point. Helene’s surface is bright and icy, showing evidence of past impacts but little geological activity.
bodies.discoveredBy: Pierre Laques, Raymond Despiau, Jean Lecacheux

bodies.name: Télesto
bodies.description: Telesto is a small Trojan moon of Saturn, sharing its orbit with Tethys at the leading Lagrange point. Measuring about 24 km across, its surface is smooth and icy. Its orbit is stabilized by Tethys’ gravity.
bodies.discoveredBy: Bradford A. Smith, Harold J. Reitsema, Stephen M. Larson, John W. Fountain

bodies.name: Calypso
bodies.description: Calypso is a small, icy moon of Saturn that shares its orbit with the larger moon Tethys, residing at Tethys’s Lagrange point. Measuring about 21 km across, its surface is heavily cratered and reflects a lot of sunlight. Calypso’s motion is stabilized by the gravitational pull of Tethys and Saturn.
bodies.discoveredBy: Dan Pascu, P. Kenneth Seidelmann, William A. Baum, Douglas Currie

bodies.name: Atlas
bodies.description: Atlas is a small, pancake-shaped moon that orbits just outside Saturn’s A-ring. Its equatorial ridge is thought to be accumulated ring material. Atlas helps shepherd the outer edge of Saturn’s rings, maintaining their sharp boundary.
bodies.discoveredBy: Richard J. Terrile

bodies.name: Prométhée
bodies.description: Prometheus is a small, inner moon of Saturn, about 86 km across, that acts as a shepherd for the inner edge of the F-ring. Its irregular shape and cratered surface suggest minimal geological activity. Prometheus’ gravitational influence helps maintain the structure and narrow confinement of Saturn’s F-ring.
bodies.discoveredBy: Stewart A. Collins, D. Carlson

bodies.name: Pandore
bodies.description: Pandora is a small moon, roughly 81 km in diameter, acting as an outer shepherd of Saturn’s F-ring. Its irregular shape and cratered surface indicate minimal geological activity. Pandora’s gravitational influence helps maintain the structure of the F-ring.
bodies.discoveredBy: Stewart A. Collins

bodies.name: Pan
bodies.description: Pan is a small, walnut-shaped moon about 35 km across, embedded within Saturn’s Encke Gap in the A-ring. Its gravity maintains the gap and shapes nearby ring material into waves. Pan’s equatorial ridge is likely formed from ring debris accumulation.
bodies.discoveredBy: Mark R. Showalter

bodies.name: Ymir
bodies.description: Ymir is a large retrograde outer moon of Saturn in the Norse group, about 18 km across. Its orbit is highly inclined and eccentric. Its dark, icy surface suggests it is likely a captured object from the outer Solar System.
bodies.discoveredBy: Brett J. Gladman

bodies.name: Paaliaq
bodies.description: Paaliaq is a small, irregular prograde moon in Saturn’s Inuit group, about 22 km across. Its reddish surface suggests a different composition from the Gallic and Norse groups. Paaliaq likely originated as a captured object rather than forming in situ.
bodies.discoveredBy: John J. Kavelaars, Brett J. Gladman

bodies.name: Tarvos
bodies.description: Tarvos is a prograde irregular moon of Saturn, part of the Gallic group, about 18 km in diameter. Its reddish surface suggests it may have been captured. It orbits Saturn at a moderately inclined and distant orbit.
bodies.discoveredBy: John J. Kavelaars, Brett J. Gladman

bodies.name: ljiraq
bodies.description: Ijiraq is a distant, irregular moon with a reddish surface, part of Saturn’s Inuit group. Measuring about 12 km across, its orbit is prograde but inclined, suggesting it may be a captured object. Its color hints at a different composition than most of Saturn’s inner moons.
bodies.discoveredBy: John J. Kavelaars, Brett J. Gladman

bodies.name: Suttungr
bodies.description: Suttungr is a small, irregular outer moon of Saturn (about 7 km in diameter) that orbits the planet in a distant, retrograde, and highly inclined path. It completes one orbit in roughly 1,017 Earth days at an average distance of about 19.5 million km, and its eccentric orbit suggests it was likely captured rather than formed in place. As a member of the Norse group of irregular satellites, Suttungr shares characteristics with other distant, dark, and irregular moons of Saturn
bodies.discoveredBy: John J. Kavelaars, Brett J. Gladman

bodies.name: Kiviuq
bodies.description: Kiviuq is a small, irregular prograde moon of Saturn, belonging to the Inuit group. Measuring around 16 km, its orbit is moderately inclined and eccentric. Its surface is relatively dark, hinting at a carbon-rich composition.
bodies.discoveredBy: Brett J. Gladman

bodies.name: Mundilfari
bodies.description: Mundilfari is a small, irregular retrograde moon in the Norse group, roughly 7 km across. Its distant and inclined orbit suggests it was captured. Its dark, icy surface reflects little sunlight.
bodies.discoveredBy: John J. Kavelaars, Brett J. Gladman

bodies.name: Albiorix
bodies.description: Albiorix is the largest member of Saturn’s Gallic group of moons, roughly 32 km in diameter. Its reddish surface and irregular orbit suggest it may be a fragment of a larger progenitor moon. Albiorix’s rotation period is about 13 hours.
bodies.discoveredBy: John J. Kavelaars, Brett J. Gladman

bodies.name: Skathi
bodies.description: Skathi is a small, irregular retrograde moon in the Norse group, roughly 6 km across. Its orbit is distant, eccentric, and inclined, indicating it was likely captured. Its dark surface reflects little sunlight.
bodies.discoveredBy: John J. Kavelaars, Brett J. Gladman

bodies.name: Erriapo
bodies.description: Erriapus is an irregular, retrograde outer moon belonging to Saturn’s Gallic group. Measuring around 10 km across, it has a dark, reddish surface. Its orbit is highly inclined, indicating it was likely captured rather than formed in place.
bodies.discoveredBy: John J. Kavelaars, Brett J. Gladman

bodies.name: Siarnaq
bodies.description: Siarnaq is a prograde irregular moon of Saturn, part of the Inuit group, about 40 km in diameter. Its reddish surface suggests a unique composition. It orbits at a considerable distance with moderate eccentricity.
bodies.discoveredBy: John J. Kavelaars, Brett J. Gladman

bodies.name: Thrymr
bodies.description: Thrymr is a small, irregular retrograde outer moon in the Norse group, approximately 7 km across. Its orbit is distant, eccentric, and highly inclined. Its dark surface reflects very little sunlight.
bodies.discoveredBy: John J. Kavelaars, Brett J. Gladman

bodies.name: Narvi
bodies.description: Narvi is a tiny, irregular moon of Saturn in the Norse group, about 7 km in diameter. It follows a retrograde and highly eccentric orbit. Its dark surface is characteristic of outer captured moons.
bodies.discoveredBy: Scott S. Sheppard, David C. Jewitt, Jan Kleyna

bodies.name: Méthone
bodies.description: Methone is a tiny, egg-shaped moon, only about 3 km across, located between Mimas and Enceladus. Its smooth surface is unusually free of craters, possibly due to a coating of fine icy material. Methone orbits Saturn within a narrow arc of ring debris.
bodies.discoveredBy: Carolyn C. Porco, Sébastien Charnoz

bodies.name: Pallène
bodies.description: Pallene is a small, irregular moon about 4 km across, orbiting between Mimas and Enceladus. It is embedded in a faint ring of material likely ejected from its surface. Pallene’s orbit is relatively stable, though slightly perturbed by nearby moons.
bodies.discoveredBy: Carolyn C. Porco, Sébastien Charnoz

bodies.name: Pollux
bodies.description: Polydeuces is a tiny Trojan moon of Saturn, sharing its orbit with Dione at the trailing Lagrange point. Measuring about 3 km in diameter, its surface is icy and cratered. Its orbital position is stabilized by Dione’s gravity.
bodies.discoveredBy: Carolyn C. Porco

bodies.name: Daphnis
bodies.description: Daphnis is a tiny moon, roughly 8 km in diameter, embedded in the Keeler Gap of Saturn’s A-ring. Its gravity creates wave patterns in the surrounding ring material, visible in images from Cassini. Daphnis has a slightly elongated shape and a rotation synchronized with its orbit.
bodies.discoveredBy: Carolyn C. Porco

bodies.name: Aegir
bodies.description: Aegir is a small, irregularly shaped moon discovered in 2004. It orbits Saturn in a retrograde, distant path, likely captured rather than formed in place. Its dark surface reflects very little sunlight, making it challenging to study.
bodies.discoveredBy: Scott S. Sheppard, David C. Jewitt, Jan Kleyna, Brian G. Marsden

bodies.name: Bebhionn
bodies.description: Bebhionn is an irregular, outer moon with a retrograde orbit around Saturn. Measuring roughly 6 km across, it likely formed elsewhere and was captured by Saturn’s gravity. Its surface is very dark and heavily cratered.
bodies.discoveredBy: Scott S. Sheppard, David C. Jewitt, Jan Kleyna, Brian G. Marsden

bodies.name: Bergelmir
bodies.description: Bergelmir is a small, retrograde moon discovered in 2004. It is part of Saturn’s Norse group of outer moons, with a diameter of about 6 km. Its orbit is highly inclined and eccentric, characteristic of captured bodies.
bodies.discoveredBy: Scott S. Sheppard, David C. Jewitt, Jan Kleyna, Brian G. Marsden

bodies.name: Bestla
bodies.description: Bestla is another small, irregular moon in the Norse group, measuring roughly 7 km. Its retrograde orbit is distant and eccentric. Like many of Saturn’s outer moons, Bestla may be a captured object from the Kuiper Belt.
bodies.discoveredBy: Scott S. Sheppard, David C. Jewitt, Jan Kleyna, Brian G. Marsden

bodies.name: Farbauti
bodies.description: Farbauti is a small, irregular moon of Saturn, roughly 5 km across, in the Norse group of retrograde satellites. Its dark, icy surface reflects very little sunlight. Its distant and eccentric orbit suggests it is a captured object.
bodies.discoveredBy: Scott S. Sheppard, David C. Jewitt, Jan Kleyna, Brian G. Marsden

bodies.name: Fenrir
bodies.description: Fenrir is a tiny, retrograde outer moon of Saturn, part of the Norse group. It measures about 4 km in diameter and has a highly eccentric orbit. Fenrir’s origin is likely a captured asteroid or Kuiper Belt object.
bodies.discoveredBy: Scott S. Sheppard, David C. Jewitt, Jan Kleyna, Brian G. Marsden

bodies.name: Fornjot
bodies.description: Fornjot is one of Saturn’s outermost moons, with a retrograde orbit and a diameter around 6 km. Its dark surface and distant orbit suggest a captured origin. Fornjot is part of the Norse group of irregular moons.
bodies.discoveredBy: Scott S. Sheppard, David C. Jewitt, Jan Kleyna, Brian G. Marsden

bodies.name: Hati
bodies.description: Hati is a small, retrograde outer moon of Saturn in the Norse group, roughly 6 km across. Its dark surface absorbs most sunlight, making it difficult to observe. Hati’s highly inclined and eccentric orbit suggests it was captured rather than formed in place.
bodies.discoveredBy: Scott S. Sheppard, David C. Jewitt, Jan Kleyna, Brian G. Marsden

bodies.name: Hyrrokkin
bodies.description: Hyrrokkin is a retrograde outer moon of Saturn in the Norse group, approximately 8 km in diameter. Its orbit is distant, inclined, and eccentric, characteristic of captured moons. Its surface is dark and likely icy, reflecting minimal sunlight.
bodies.discoveredBy: Scott S. Sheppard, David C. Jewitt, Jan Kleyna, Brian G. Marsden

bodies.name: Kari
bodies.description: Kari is a small, irregular, retrograde outer moon in the Norse group, about 6 km in diameter. Its distant and eccentric orbit indicates it was likely captured. Its surface is dark and unremarkable, typical of Saturn’s outer irregular moons.
bodies.discoveredBy: Scott S. Sheppard, David C. Jewitt, Jan Kleyna, Brian G. Marsden

bodies.name: Loge
bodies.description: Loge is a small retrograde outer moon in the Norse group, approximately 6 km in diameter. Its orbit is distant, highly inclined, and eccentric, typical of captured bodies. The moon’s surface is dark and featureless.
bodies.discoveredBy: Scott S. Sheppard, David C. Jewitt, Jan Kleyna, Brian G. Marsden

bodies.name: Skoll
bodies.description: Skoll is a tiny retrograde moon of Saturn in the Norse group, measuring about 6 km. Its orbit is highly inclined and eccentric, typical of captured outer moons. The surface is dark and icy.
bodies.discoveredBy: Scott S. Sheppard, David C. Jewitt, Jan Kleyna, Brian G. Marsden

bodies.name: Surtur
bodies.description: Surtur is a small, irregular retrograde moon in the Norse group, around 6 km across. It has a distant, eccentric orbit with a high inclination. Its dark surface is similar to other outer irregular moons.
bodies.discoveredBy: Scott S. Sheppard, David C. Jewitt, Jan Kleyna, Brian G. Marsden

bodies.name: Anthé
bodies.description: Anthe is a tiny moon about 1 km in diameter, residing in a faint ring arc near Mimas. Its orbit is influenced by resonances with Mimas, causing the ring material around it to form arcs. Anthe’s surface is likely icy and irregular.
bodies.discoveredBy: Carolyn C. Porco, l'équipe Cassini

bodies.name: Jarnsaxa
bodies.description: Jarnsaxa is a small (≈ 4–6 km) irregular outer moon of Saturn, discovered in 2006. It orbits in a distant, retrograde, highly inclined and eccentric orbit, completing one orbit roughly every 943–965 days. As a member of Saturn’s Norse group of irregular moons, its orbital characteristics suggest it was likely a captured object rather than having formed in place.
bodies.discoveredBy: Scott S. Sheppard, David C. Jewitt, Jan Kleyna, Brian G. Marsden

bodies.name: Greip
bodies.description: Greip is a small, irregular moon in the Norse group, measuring roughly 6 km across. Its orbit is retrograde, highly inclined, and eccentric. Like many of Saturn’s distant moons, Greip is likely a captured object from elsewhere in the Solar System.
bodies.discoveredBy: Scott S. Sheppard, David C. Jewitt, Jan Kleyna, Brian G. Marsden

bodies.name: Tarqeq
bodies.description: Tarqeq is a small, irregular outer moon of Saturn, about 7 km in diameter, discovered in 2007 and designated S/2007 S 1. It orbits Saturn at an average distance of about 17.9 million km in a prograde, inclined, and slightly eccentric orbit with a period of roughly 895 days. As a member of Saturn’s Inuit group of irregular moons, Tarqeq’s similar orbital characteristics to other Inuit moons suggest it may be a fragment of a larger body that was captured by Saturn’s gravity.
bodies.discoveredBy: Scott S. Sheppard, David C. Jewitt, Jan Kleyna, Brian G. Marsden

bodies.name: Égéon
bodies.description: Aegaeon is a tiny moon embedded within Saturn’s G-ring, measuring only about 0.5 km across. Its irregular shape and dusty surface suggest it may be a captured fragment from a larger body. Aegaeon’s orbit is strongly influenced by Saturn’s larger moons, particularly Mimas.
bodies.discoveredBy: Carolyn C. Porco

bodies.name: S/2004 S 7
bodies.description: S/2004 S 7 is a small, irregular outer moon of Saturn, discovered in 2004. Its retrograde orbit suggests it is a captured object. Measuring only a few kilometers across, it reflects very little sunlight.
bodies.discoveredBy: Scott S. Sheppard, David C. Jewitt, Jan Kleyna, Brian G. Marsden

bodies.name: S/2004 S 12
bodies.description: S/2004 S 12 is another tiny, distant moon in Saturn’s Norse group. Its retrograde, eccentric orbit indicates a captured origin. Its diameter is estimated at just a few kilometers.
bodies.discoveredBy: Scott S. Sheppard, David C. Jewitt, Jan Kleyna, Brian G. Marsden

bodies.name: S/2004 S 13
bodies.description: S/2004 S 13 is a minor irregular moon of Saturn, part of the Norse group, orbiting in a retrograde direction. Its small size and dark surface make it difficult to observe. Its orbit is distant and highly inclined.
bodies.discoveredBy: Scott S. Sheppard, David C. Jewitt, Jan Kleyna, Brian G. Marsden

bodies.name: S/2004 S 17
bodies.description: S/2004 S 17 is a small, irregular retrograde moon in Saturn’s outer system. Its distant orbit is eccentric and highly inclined. Like other outer moons, it likely originated as a captured object.
bodies.discoveredBy: Scott S. Sheppard, David C. Jewitt, Jan Kleyna, Brian G. Marsden

bodies.name: S/2006 S 1
bodies.description: S/2006 S 1 is a small outer moon discovered in 2006. It has a retrograde, eccentric orbit in the Norse group. Its size is only a few kilometers across.
bodies.discoveredBy: Scott S. Sheppard, David C. Jewitt, Jan Kleyna, Brian G. Marsden

bodies.name: S/2006 S 3
bodies.description: S/2006 S 3 is a tiny, irregular retrograde moon in Saturn’s Norse group. Its distant orbit is inclined and eccentric. Like other outer moons, it is likely a captured object.
bodies.discoveredBy: Scott S. Sheppard, David C. Jewitt, Jan Kleyna, Brian G. Marsden

bodies.name: S/2007 S 2
bodies.description: S/2007 S 2 is a minor, irregular outer moon of Saturn. Its retrograde orbit is distant and eccentric. Its surface is small, dark, and icy.
bodies.discoveredBy: Scott S. Sheppard, David C. Jewitt, Jan Kleyna, Brian G. Marsden

bodies.name: S/2007 S 3
bodies.description: S/2007 S 3 is a tiny, irregular moon in the Norse group. It has a retrograde, highly inclined orbit. Its size is only a few kilometers, making it very faint.
bodies.discoveredBy: Scott S. Sheppard, David C. Jewitt, Jan Kleyna, Brian G. Marsden

bodies.name: S/2009 S 1
bodies.description: S/2009 S 1 is a tiny moonlet of Saturn embedded in the outer part of the B Ring, discovered by the Cassini spacecraft in 2009 during an equinox when it cast a long shadow over the dense ring. With an estimated diameter of about 0.3 km, it is one of the smallest known natural satellites in the Solar System and orbits very close to Saturn at roughly 117 000 km from the planet’s center. Its orbit is nearly circular and lies within the ring plane, making it distinct from most of the distant irregular moons
bodies.discoveredBy: Carolyn C. Porco

bodies.name: S/2004 S 3
bodies.description: S/2004 S 3 is a tiny, irregular outer moon of Saturn, only a few kilometers across. It follows a distant, retrograde, and highly inclined orbit, characteristic of captured moons in the Norse group. Its small size and dark surface make it very faint and difficult to observe.
bodies.discoveredBy: Carl Murray

bodies.name: S/2004 S 4
bodies.description: S/2004 S 4 was initially reported as a possible small moon just outside the F ring of Saturn. Its existence remains uncertain, and it may have been a transient clump of ring material rather than a stable moon
bodies.discoveredBy: Carl Murray

bodies.name: S/2004 S 6
bodies.description: S/2004 S 6 was initially reported near Saturn’s F ring, with uncertain existence. It may have been a transient clump of ring material rather than a stable moon
bodies.discoveredBy: Carolyn C. Porco

bodies.name: Angrboda
bodies.description: Angrboda is a small, irregular outer moon of Saturn (≈ 3 km in diameter), orbiting in a retrograde, highly inclined orbit at ~20.6 million km from Saturn. It completes one orbit roughly every 1107 days, and its eccentric, distant orbit suggests it was likely captured rather than formed in place. Angrboda belongs to Saturn’s Norse group of irregular moons and is one of the smallest known named Saturnian moons.
bodies.discoveredBy: S. Sheppard, D. Jewitt, J. Kleyna

bodies.name: S/2004 S 21
bodies.description: S/2004 S 21 is a minor moon in Saturn’s Norse group. Its orbit is retrograde, eccentric, and distant from the planet. The moon is only a few kilometers across.
bodies.discoveredBy: S. Sheppard, D. Jewitt, J. Kleyna

bodies.name: Gridr
bodies.description: Gridr is a tiny, irregularly shaped outer moon of Saturn. It orbits in a retrograde, highly inclined path. Its surface is dark and icy, reflecting minimal sunlight.
bodies.discoveredBy: S. Sheppard, D. Jewitt, J. Kleyna

bodies.name: Skrymir
bodies.description: Skrymir is a tiny outer moon with a retrograde, irregular orbit. Its small size and dark surface make it hard to observe. Like most outer moons, it was likely captured.
bodies.discoveredBy: S. Sheppard, D. Jewitt, J. Kleyna

bodies.name: S/2004 S 24
bodies.description: S/2004 S 24 is a small, retrograde outer moon of Saturn in the Norse group. Its orbit is distant, eccentric, and inclined. Its dark, icy surface is typical of captured moons.
bodies.discoveredBy: S. Sheppard, D. Jewitt, J. Kleyna

bodies.name: Gerd
bodies.description: Gerd is a minor, irregular moon with a retrograde orbit. Its small size and distant orbit suggest it is a captured object. Its surface is dark and icy.
bodies.discoveredBy: S. Sheppard, D. Jewitt, J. Kleyna

bodies.name: S/2004 S 26
bodies.description: S/2004 S 26 is a small, irregular outer moon of Saturn, about 4 km in diameter. It orbits Saturn in a distant, retrograde, highly‑inclined and eccentric orbit with a period of roughly 1624 days, making it one of the most distant known moons of the planet. As part of Saturn’s Norse group of irregular satellites, its remote and erratic orbit strongly suggests it was captured rather than formed in place.
bodies.discoveredBy: S. Sheppard, D. Jewitt, J. Kleyna

bodies.name: Eggther
bodies.description: Eggther is a small, irregular outer moon of Saturn (≈ 6 km in diameter). It orbits Saturn in a distant, retrograde, highly inclined orbit with a period of about 1054 days. As a member of Saturn’s Norse group of moons, its eccentric and distant orbit suggests it was likely a captured object rather than having formed in place.
bodies.discoveredBy: S. Sheppard, D. Jewitt, J. Kleyna

bodies.name: S/2004 S 28
bodies.description: S/2004 S 28 is a small (~4 km diameter) irregular outer moon of Saturn. It follows a distant, retrograde, highly inclined orbit at an average distance of about 22 million km and completes one orbit in roughly 1220 days. As a member of the Norse group of irregular moons, its eccentric, inclined, and distant orbit strongly suggests it was captured rather than formed alongside Saturn’s regular moons.
bodies.discoveredBy: S. Sheppard, D. Jewitt, J. Kleyna

bodies.name: S/2004 S 29
bodies.description: S/2004 S 29 is a small, irregular outer moon of Saturn, about 4 km in diameter. It orbits Saturn on a distant, prograde, inclined and eccentric orbit with a period of roughly 838 days and a semi‑major axis near 17 million km. As part of the planet’s outer irregular satellites (now classified in the Gallic group), its remote orbit and small size suggest it was likely captured rather than formed in place.
bodies.discoveredBy: S. Sheppard, D. Jewitt, J. Kleyna

bodies.name: Beli
bodies.description: Beli is a small, irregular outer moon of Saturn, about 4 km in diameter. It orbits retrograde at a large distance (~20.4 million km) with a period of ≈1087 days and an orbital eccentricity of ~0.113, suggesting it was likely captured rather than formed in place. It belongs to Saturn’s Norse group of moons.
bodies.discoveredBy: S. Sheppard, D. Jewitt, J. Kleyna

bodies.name: S/2004 S 31
bodies.description: S/2004 S 31 is a small outer moon of Saturn in the Inuit group, about 4 km in diameter. It orbits at roughly 17.57 million km with a period of ≈ 854 days, at an inclination of about 48° and eccentricity ≈ 0.24. Its prograde, inclined orbit suggests it is an irregular satellite, likely captured rather than formed in place
bodies.discoveredBy: S. Sheppard, D. Jewitt, J. Kleyna

bodies.name: Gunnlod
bodies.description: Gunnlod is a small, irregular outer moon of Saturn (≈ 4 km in diameter). It orbits Saturn in a distant, retrograde orbit with a semi‑major axis around 21.2 million km, an orbital period of roughly 1154 days, eccentricity ~ 0.25 and inclination ~ 159°, placing it in the Norse group. Its orbital characteristics suggest it was likely captured rather than forming with Saturn’s main moons.
bodies.discoveredBy: S. Sheppard, D. Jewitt, J. Kleyna

bodies.name: Thiazzi
bodies.description: Thiazzi is a small, irregular outer moon of Saturn, roughly 4 km in diameter. It orbits Saturn in a retrograde, inclined, and quite eccentric orbit with a period around 1361 days, placing it among the distant Norse-group satellites. Its remote and irregular orbit indicates a likely captured origin.
bodies.discoveredBy: S. Sheppard, D. Jewitt, J. Kleyna

bodies.name: S/2004 S 34
bodies.description: S/2004 S 34 is a tiny, irregular outer moon of Saturn, ~3 km across. It orbits at an average distance of ~24.30 million km, in a retrograde orbit with an inclination of ~166° and eccentricity ≈ 0.235, completing one orbit in about 1,414 days. As part of the Norse group, its distant, inclined, and eccentric orbit suggests a captured origin
bodies.discoveredBy: S. Sheppard, D. Jewitt, J. Kleyna

bodies.name: Alvaldi
bodies.description: Alvaldi is a small, irregular, outer moon of Saturn, discovered in 2004. Its retrograde orbit indicates it was likely captured rather than formed with the planet. Alvaldi’s diameter is estimated at just 5 km.
bodies.discoveredBy: S. Sheppard, D. Jewitt, J. Kleyna

bodies.name: S/2004 S 36
bodies.description: S/2004 S 36 is an irregular outer moon of Saturn, with a diameter of ~3 km. It travels in a highly eccentric (e ≈ 0.667), retrograde orbit at an average distance of ~23.2 million km, taking roughly 1,319 days to complete an orbit. Its extreme eccentricity and remote orbit mark it as a likely captured object in the Norse group
bodies.discoveredBy: S. Sheppard, D. Jewitt, J. Kleyna

bodies.name: S/2004 S 37
bodies.description: S/2004 S 37 is a small (~4 km) retrograde outer moon of Saturn. It orbits at about 15.9 million km with a period of ~753 days, inclination ~164°, and eccentricity ~0.506. Its distant, inclined, and eccentric orbit is consistent with a captured irregular moon from the Norse group
bodies.discoveredBy: S. Sheppard, D. Jewitt, J. Kleyna

bodies.name: Geirrod
bodies.description: Geirrod is a small, irregular outer moon of Saturn with an estimated diameter of about 4 km. It orbits Saturn at a large distance (~21.9 million km) in a retrograde, eccentric orbit lasting roughly 1211 days. As a member of the Norse group, its characteristics strongly suggest it was captured after formation elsewhere.
bodies.discoveredBy: S. Sheppard, D. Jewitt, J. Kleyna

bodies.name: S/2004 S 39
bodies.description: S/2004 S 39 is a very small (~2–3 km) irregular outer moon of Saturn. It orbits around ~23.6 million km from Saturn, with an orbital period of ~1,352 days, inclination ≈ 167°, and a low eccentricity (~0.08). As part of Saturn’s outer Norse-group satellites, its remote, retrograde orbit strongly suggests a captured origin
bodies.discoveredBy: S. Sheppard, D. Jewitt, J. Kleyna

bodies.name: S/2004 S 41
bodies.description: S/2004 S 41 is a tiny, irregular outer moon of Saturn, roughly 4 km in diameter. It follows a retrograde, eccentric, and inclined orbit as part of the Norse group, indicating it was likely captured
bodies.discoveredBy: S.S. Sheppard, D.C. Jewitt, J. Kleyna, E.J. Ashton, B.J. Gladman, J.-M. Petit, M. Alexandersen

bodies.name: S/2004 S 40
bodies.description: S/2004 S 40 is a small, irregular retrograde moon of Saturn. Its diameter is estimated around 4 km, and it orbits at a large distance in the Norse group with high inclination and eccentricity. Its orbit suggests a captured origin
bodies.discoveredBy: S.S. Sheppard, D.C. Jewitt, J. Kleyna

bodies.name: S/2004 S 42
bodies.description: S/2004 S 42 is a small, irregular outer moon of Saturn, around 4 km across. Its orbit is retrograde, eccentric, and highly inclined, characteristic of the Norse group of captured satellites
bodies.discoveredBy: S.S. Sheppard, D.C. Jewitt, J. Kleyna, E.J. Ashton, B.J. Gladman, J.-M. Petit, M. Alexandersen

bodies.name: S/2004 S 43
bodies.description: S/2004 S 43 is a small (~4 km) retrograde irregular moon of Saturn. It orbits in the Norse group at a distant, inclined, and eccentric trajectory, suggesting a captured origin
bodies.discoveredBy: S.S. Sheppard, D.C. Jewitt, J. Kleyna, E.J. Ashton, B.J. Gladman, J.-M. Petit, M. Alexandersen

bodies.name: S/2004 S 44
bodies.description: S/2004 S 44 is a small, irregular retrograde outer moon of Saturn, roughly 4 km in diameter. Its orbit is highly inclined and eccentric, typical of captured Norse-group moons
bodies.discoveredBy: S.S. Sheppard, D.C. Jewitt, J. Kleyna, E.J. Ashton, B.J. Gladman

bodies.name: S/2004 S 45
bodies.description: S/2004 S 45 is a small (~4 km) retrograde outer moon of Saturn. Its orbit is distant, eccentric, and highly inclined, making it a typical Norse-group captured moon
bodies.discoveredBy: D.C. Jewitt, S.S. Sheppard, J. Kleyna, E.J. Ashton, B.J. Gladman, J.-M. Petit, M. Alexandersen

bodies.name: S/2004 S 46
bodies.description: S/2004 S 46 is a tiny, irregular outer moon of Saturn. It follows a retrograde, eccentric, and inclined orbit, consistent with the Norse group of captured satellites
bodies.discoveredBy: S.S. Sheppard, D.C. Jewitt, E.J. Ashton, B.J. Gladman, J.-M. Petit, M. Alexandersen

bodies.name: S/2004 S 47
bodies.description: S/2004 S 47 is a small, irregular retrograde moon of Saturn, about 4 km across. Its orbit is distant, eccentric, and inclined, characteristic of captured Norse-group moons
bodies.discoveredBy: S.S. Sheppard, D.C. Jewitt, J. Kleyna, E.J. Ashton, B.J. Gladman, J.-M. Petit, M. Alexandersen

bodies.name: S/2004 S 48
bodies.description: S/2004 S 48 is a small, retrograde irregular outer moon of Saturn, roughly 4 km in diameter. Its distant, inclined, and eccentric orbit places it in the Norse group, likely captured
bodies.discoveredBy: D.C. Jewitt, S.S. Sheppard, E.J. Ashton, B.J. Gladman, J.-M. Petit, M. Alexandersen

bodies.name: S/2004 S 49
bodies.description: S/2004 S 49 is a small, irregular outer moon of Saturn (~4 km). Its orbit is retrograde, highly inclined, and eccentric, typical of the Norse group and suggesting a captured origin
bodies.discoveredBy: S.S. Sheppard, D.C. Jewitt, J. Kleyna, E.J. Ashton, B.J. Gladman, J.-M. Petit, M. Alexandersen

bodies.name: S/2004 S 50
bodies.description: S/2004 S 50 is a tiny (~4 km) retrograde outer moon of Saturn. Its distant, eccentric, and inclined orbit indicates a captured origin in the Norse group
bodies.discoveredBy: S.S. Sheppard, D.C. Jewitt, E.J. Ashton, B.J. Gladman, J.-M. Petit, M. Alexandersen

bodies.name: S/2004 S 51
bodies.description: S/2004 S 51 is a small (~4 km) irregular retrograde moon of Saturn. Its orbit is distant, highly inclined, and eccentric, consistent with a captured Norse-group satellite
bodies.discoveredBy: S.S. Sheppard, D.C. Jewitt, J. Kleyna, E.J. Ashton, B.J. Gladman, J.-M. Petit, M. Alexandersen

bodies.name: S/2004 S 52
bodies.description: S/2004 S 52 is a small (~4 km) retrograde irregular moon of Saturn. Its orbit is distant, eccentric, and inclined, typical of captured Norse-group moons
bodies.discoveredBy: D.C. Jewitt, S.S. Sheppard, E.J. Ashton, B.J. Gladman, J.-M. Petit, M. Alexandersen

bodies.name: S/2004 S 53
bodies.description: S/2004 S 53 is a tiny, irregular retrograde outer moon of Saturn. Its distant, inclined, and eccentric orbit marks it as part of the Norse group, likely captured
bodies.discoveredBy: S.S. Sheppard, D.C. Jewitt, J. Kleyna, E.J. Ashton, B.J. Gladman, J.-M. Petit, M. Alexandersen

bodies.name: S/2005 S 4
bodies.description: S/2005 S 4 is a small (~4 km) irregular outer moon of Saturn discovered in 2005 from ground‑based observations and announced in 2023. It orbits Saturn in a prograde, inclined, and eccentric path at an average distance of about 11.33 million km, completing one orbit in roughly 448.6 days. As a member of the Inuit group of irregular moons, its orbital characteristics suggest it was likely captured rather than having formed in place
bodies.discoveredBy: S.S. Sheppard, D.C. Jewitt, J. Kleyna, E.J. Ashton, B.J. Gladman, J.-M. Petit, M. Alexandersen

bodies.name: S/2005 S 5
bodies.description: S/2005 S 5 is a small (~3 km) irregular outer moon of Saturn discovered from observations made between 2005 and 2020, and its existence was announced in 2023. It orbits Saturn at an average distance of about 21.0 million km in a distant, retrograde, highly inclined orbit with an orbital period of roughly 1,139 days. As a member of the Norse group of irregular satellites, its orbit is consistent with a captured origin rather than having formed in place
bodies.discoveredBy: S.S. Sheppard, D.C. Jewitt, J. Kleyna, E.J. Ashton, B.J. Gladman, J.-M. Petit, M. Alexandersen

bodies.name: S/2006 S 10
bodies.description: S/2006 S 10 is a very small (~2 km) irregular outer moon of Saturn discovered in 2006 and announced in 2023 from long‑term observations. It orbits Saturn in a distant, retrograde, highly inclined orbit with a semi‑major axis of about 18.8 million km, an inclination near 161°, and an orbital period of about 965 days. As part of the Norse group of irregular satellites, its remote, eccentric, and inclined orbit suggests it was likely captured rather than formed in place
bodies.discoveredBy: S.S. Sheppard, D.C. Jewitt, J. Kleyna, E.J. Ashton, B.J. Gladman, J.-M. Petit, M. Alexandersen

bodies.name: S/2006 S 11
bodies.description: S/2006 S 11 is a small (~2 km) irregular outer moon of Saturn discovered in 2006 and announced in 2023. It orbits Saturn in a distant, retrograde, highly inclined, and moderately eccentric orbit with a period of roughly 1,018 days, indicating a captured origin. Its orbit and small size place it among the Norse group of irregular satellites
bodies.discoveredBy: S.S. Sheppard, D.C. Jewitt, J. Kleyna, E.J. Ashton, B.J. Gladman, J.-M. Petit, M. Alexandersen

bodies.name: S/2006 S 12
bodies.description: S/2006 S 12 is a small (~4 km) irregular outer moon of Saturn orbiting at about 19.6 million km with a period of roughly 1,043 days. Its orbit is prograde, moderately inclined (~39°) and eccentric, and it may be related to the Gallic group of irregular moons. Its distant, inclined path suggests it was likely captured rather than formed in place
bodies.discoveredBy: S.S. Sheppard, D.C. Jewitt, J. Kleyna, E.J. Ashton, B.J. Gladman, J.-M. Petit, M. Alexandersen

bodies.name: S/2006 S 13
bodies.description: S/2006 S 13 is a small irregular outer moon of Saturn, orbiting at an intermediate distance among the distant satellites. Its retrograde, highly inclined, and eccentric orbit with a multi‑year period indicates it was likely captured rather than formed with the regular moons. As with many of the S/2006 discoveries, its small size (a few kilometers) and remote orbit mark it as a Norse‑group irregular satellite
bodies.discoveredBy: S.S. Sheppard, D.C. Jewitt, J. Kleyna, E.J. Ashton, B.J. Gladman, J.-M. Petit, M. Alexandersen

bodies.name: S/2006 S 14
bodies.description: S/2006 S 14 is a small irregular outer moon of Saturn that travels in a distant, retrograde orbit with significant inclination. Its long orbital period and eccentric trajectory are typical of the Norse group of captured satellites. With an estimated diameter of a few kilometers, its surface is likely dark and heavily cratered
bodies.discoveredBy: S.S. Sheppard, D.C. Jewitt, J. Kleyna, E.J. Ashton, B.J. Gladman, J.-M. Petit, M. Alexandersen

bodies.name: S/2006 S 15
bodies.description: S/2006 S 15 is a small (~2–3 km) irregular outer moon of Saturn with a retrograde, inclined orbit. It completes an orbit in over three years and its eccentric path suggests it was likely captured from a passing body. It is part of Saturn’s Norse group of distant irregular satellites
bodies.discoveredBy: S.S. Sheppard, D.C. Jewitt, J. Kleyna, E.J. Ashton, B.J. Gladman

bodies.name: S/2006 S 16
bodies.description: S/2006 S 16 is a small irregular outer moon of Saturn with a distant, retrograde, and highly inclined orbit. Its long orbital period and eccentric trajectory indicate capture rather than formation in situ. It belongs to the Norse group of Saturn’s irregular satellites
bodies.discoveredBy: S.S. Sheppard, D.C. Jewitt, J. Kleyna, E.J. Ashton, B.J. Gladman, J.-M. Petit, M. Alexandersen

bodies.name: S/2006 S 17
bodies.description: S/2006 S 17 is a small (~3 km) irregular outer moon of Saturn that follows a distant, retrograde, and inclined orbit with a period of over three years. Its eccentric and remote path suggests it was captured rather than formed with Saturn’s larger moons. It is classified within the Norse group of irregular satellites
bodies.discoveredBy: S.S. Sheppard, D.C. Jewitt, J. Kleyna, E.J. Ashton, B.J. Gladman

bodies.name: S/2006 S 18
bodies.description: S/2006 S 18 is a small irregular outer moon of Saturn, orbiting at a distant, retrograde path with high inclination. Its long orbital period and eccentric trajectory indicate it was likely a captured body. It is part of Saturn’s Norse group of irregular satellites
bodies.discoveredBy: S.S. Sheppard, D.C. Jewitt, J. Kleyna, E.J. Ashton, B.J. Gladman, J.-M. Petit, M. Alexandersen

bodies.name: S/2006 S 19
bodies.description: S/2006 S 19 is a tiny irregular outer moon of Saturn with a distant, retrograde, and highly inclined orbit. Its multi‑year orbital period and eccentric path suggest a captured origin. As with other distant moons, its small size and remote orbit place it among the Norse group
bodies.discoveredBy: S.S. Sheppard, D.C. Jewitt, J. Kleyna, E.J. Ashton, B.J. Gladman, J.-M. Petit, M. Alexandersen

bodies.name: S/2006 S 20
bodies.description: S/2006 S 20 is a small irregular outer moon of Saturn, orbiting far from the planet on a retrograde, inclined orbit. Its long orbital period and eccentric path point to a captured origin rather than in‑situ formation. It is part of the Norse group of distant Saturnian satellites
bodies.discoveredBy: S.S. Sheppard, D.C. Jewitt, J. Kleyna, E.J. Ashton, B.J. Gladman, J.-M. Petit, M. Alexandersen

bodies.name: S/2007 S 5
bodies.description: S/2005 S 5 is a small (~3 km) irregular outer moon of Saturn in the Norse group, discovered in observations from 2005 that were confirmed and announced in 2023. It orbits at about 21.03 million km from Saturn with a retrograde, highly inclined and eccentric path, taking roughly 1,139 days per orbit. Its orbit and physical characteristics suggest it was likely captured rather than formed in place
bodies.discoveredBy: S.S. Sheppard, D.C. Jewitt, J. Kleyna, E.J. Ashton, B.J. Gladman, J.-M. Petit, M. Alexandersen

bodies.name: S/2007 S 6
bodies.discoveredBy: S.S. Sheppard, D.C. Jewitt, J. Kleyna, E.J. Ashton, B.J. Gladman, J.-M. Petit, M. Alexandersen

bodies.name: S/2007 S 7
bodies.discoveredBy: S.S. Sheppard, D.C. Jewitt, J. Kleyna

bodies.name: S/2007 S 8
bodies.discoveredBy: S.S. Sheppard, D.C. Jewitt, J. Kleyna, E.J. Ashton, B.J. Gladman, J.-M. Petit, M. Alexandersen

bodies.name: S/2007 S 9
bodies.discoveredBy: S.S. Sheppard, D.C. Jewitt, J. Kleyna, E.J. Ashton, B.J. Gladman

bodies.name: S/2019 S 1
bodies.description: S/2019 S 1 is a small (~5 km) irregular moon of Saturn in the Inuit group with a prograde orbit, discovered from Canada–France–Hawaii Telescope data taken in 2019 and announced in 2021. It orbits Saturn at ~11.2 million km in about 444 days with a high eccentricity and moderate inclination, possibly a collision fragment related to Kiviuq and Ijiraq. Its orbit brings it unusually close among irregular moons, making it distinct in the Inuit subgroup
bodies.discoveredBy: E.J. Ashton, B.J. Gladman, J.-M. Petit, C. Veillet, M. Alexandersen

bodies.name: S/2019 S 10
bodies.discoveredBy: E.J. Ashton, B.J. Gladman, J.-M. Petit, M. Alexandersen

bodies.name: S/2019 S 11
bodies.discoveredBy: S.S. Sheppard, D.C. Jewitt, E.J. Ashton, B.J. Gladman, J.-M. Petit, M. Alexandersen

bodies.name: S/2019 S 12
bodies.discoveredBy: E.J. Ashton, B.J. Gladman

bodies.name: S/2019 S 13
bodies.description: S/2019 S 13 is an irregular outer moon of Saturn (~3 km diameter) that orbits retrograde at about 20.96 million km with a period of roughly 1,133 days. Its highly inclined and eccentric orbit is typical of Norse group satellites. It was discovered from CFHT observations in 2019 and announced in 2023 as part of the large batch of new irregular moons
bodies.discoveredBy: E.J. Ashton, B.J. Gladman, J.-M. Petit, M. Alexandersen

bodies.discoveredBy: E.J. Ashton, B.J. Gladman

bodies.discoveredBy: S.S. Sheppard, D.C. Jewitt, E.J. Ashton, B.J. Gladman, J.-M. Petit, M. Alexandersen

bodies.name: S/2019 S 19
bodies.description: S/2019 S 19 is a small (~3 km) irregular outer moon in Saturn’s Norse group, orbiting retrograde at ~23.47 million km with an orbital period of ~1,343 days. Its orbit is highly inclined and eccentric, characteristic of distant captured satellites. It was reported in 2023 based on observations taken between 2019 and 2021
bodies.discoveredBy: E.J. Ashton, B.J. Gladman

bodies.name: S/2019 S 2
bodies.description: S/2019 S 2 is a small (~3 km) irregular moon of Saturn in the distant Norse group, discovered in 2019 and announced in 2023. It has a retrograde orbit at ~16.6 million km from Saturn, taking about 799 days to complete one orbit with significant inclination and moderate eccentricity. Its orbit places it among Saturn’s numerous remote captured satellites in the Norse group
bodies.discoveredBy: E.J. Ashton, B.J. Gladman, J.-M. Petit, M. Alexandersen

bodies.name: S/2019 S 20
bodies.description: S/2019 S 20 is one of the smaller irregular moons of Saturn (~2.5 km in diameter), orbiting retrograde at ~23.5 million km with a period of about 1,343 days. Its distant, eccentric, and highly inclined orbit indicates a captured origin typical of the Norse group. It was discovered in 2019 and announced among many new Saturnian moons in 2023
bodies.discoveredBy: E.J. Ashton, B.J. Gladman, J.-M. Petit, M. Alexandersen

bodies.name: S/2019 S 21
bodies.description: S/2019 S 21 is a small (~4 km) retrograde irregular moon of Saturn, orbiting at ~26.1 million km with a period of ~1,636 days. Its highly inclined and eccentric orbit places it among Saturn’s most distant confirmed moons in the Norse group. It was announced in 2023 after analysis of data spanning 2006–2021
bodies.discoveredBy: S.S. Sheppard, D.C. Jewitt, J. Kleyna, E.J. Ashton, B.J. Gladman, J.-M. Petit, M. Alexandersen

bodies.name: S/2004 S 54
bodies.description: S/2004 S 54 is a small (~4 km) retrograde irregular moon of Saturn. Its orbit is distant, eccentric, and highly inclined, characteristic of Norse-group captured satellites
bodies.discoveredBy: Ashton, Gladman, Mike Alexandersen, Jean-Marc Petit

bodies.name: S/2004 S 55
bodies.description: S/2004 S 55 is a tiny, irregular retrograde outer moon of Saturn. Its orbit is distant, eccentric, and inclined, placing it in the Norse group and suggesting a captured origin
bodies.discoveredBy: Ashton, Gladman, Mike Alexandersen, Jean-Marc Petit
